message:
fix(packs/core/filesystem): close subshell gap in tar-remove-files-root-home + e2e + landing

Builds on bd7970a/3645389 (initial tar --remove-files coverage) by closing
the one bypass that initial regex left open and shipping the rest of the
landing-the-plane work for git_safety_guard-nqhi.6:

1. **Regex fix**: the path-tail lookahead `(?=\s|$|['"])` did not include
   `)`, so `(tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc)` — the subshell form —
   matched only the High-tier general rule, not the Critical root-home
   rule. Expanded to `(?=[\s\)'"]|$)` so the closing paren terminates a
   sensitive-path token. This is the same latent gap that find-delete /
   unlink / truncate-zero / shred sibling rules also have; tracking the
   pack-wide fix separately to keep this change scoped.

2. **E2E scenarios** (`scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`): five
   new scenario functions (`scenario_tar_remove_files_root_home`,
   `_general`, `_temp_safe`, `_no_false_positive`, `_bypass_var`) with
   55 assertions covering flag/source order-agnosticism, /dev/null
   delete-only, quoted/home variants, compound + subshell + sudo/env +
   path-prefix wrappers, mixed-source rescue rejection, and the
   DCG_BYPASS truthy/falsy contract. Replaces the
   `# scenario_tar_remove_files_*() { :; }` placeholder.

3. **Golden artifact regeneration**: keywords_checked arrays in
   destructive_git_reset.json, heredoc_python_rmtree.json, and
   safe_git_status.json picked up the new `tar`/`/tar` keywords (and
   the unlink/truncate/shred keywords from the prior nqhi children
   that hadn't regenerated their goldens at the time).

4. **CHANGELOG**: new "Recursive-force-delete bypass family" bullet
   under [Unreleased] Security Hardening summarising the closure of
   find-delete / unlink / truncate / shred / tar --remove-files as a
   single sibling-bypass family.

5. **Beads**: closes git_safety_guard-nqhi.6.

All checks green: 2695 lib tests + integration suites pass; full e2e
harness 251/251; legacy e2e_test.sh 261/261; cargo fmt + clippy clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -566,7 +566,7 @@
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.3","title":"Block unlink as recursive-force-delete equivalent for single files","description":"## Description

`unlink <file>` is the raw POSIX unlink(2) — equivalent to `rm <file>` (no `-r`, single file). Today dcg has no `unlink` coverage. Combined with a sensitive target like `/etc/passwd`, this is a one-shot data destruction primitive.

Variants:
- `unlink <file>`           — POSIX unlink builtin / GNU coreutils binary
- `python3 -c 'os.unlink("...")'` — already covered by heredoc.python rules

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"unlink"` and `"/unlink"` in `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- PATH_NORMALIZER: add `unlink` to capture group in BOTH normalizers.
- Destructive `unlink-root-home` (Critical): regex `\bunlink\s+` + sensitive path.
- Destructive `unlink-general` (High): `unlink <any-path>` not under temp.
- Safe `unlink-tmp` (whole-command anchored): `^unlink\s+/tmp/...$` and the standard temp variants.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 2 destructive + 4 safe patterns in `src/packs/core/filesystem.rs`.
- [ ] Keyword in BOTH `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated + unit-tested.
- [ ] Suggestion entries.
- [ ] No regression: `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] `scenario_unlink` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `unlink /etc/passwd`
- `unlink /etc/shadow`
- `unlink ~/.bashrc`
- `unlink $HOME/.ssh/id_ed25519`
- `/usr/bin/unlink /etc/passwd`
- `sudo unlink /etc/passwd`
- `echo done; unlink /etc/passwd`

Block (High):
- `unlink ./important.db`
- `unlink build/critical.bin`

Allow:
- `unlink /tmp/scratch.bin`
- `unlink $TMPDIR/cache.bin`
- `unlink --help`
- `cat /etc/unlink-readme.md`           (substring trap)
- `find / -newer foo -unlink-this-fake-flag`  (substring trap)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 unlink /etc/passwd` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness.)

### Out of scope

- `unlink(2)` syscalls inside compiled binaries — out of dcg scope (we block at exec, not at syscall).","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.

Added unlink to PACK_ENTRIES + PATH_NORMALIZER. Wired suggestions. 8 unit tests + 5 e2e scenarios all green. 94/94 e2e pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.3","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:02:29.508812052Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.4","title":"Block bash >|>>|>| redirect-to-sensitive-path as truncate-equivalent","description":"## Description

Bash output redirection truncates the target file:
- `> /etc/passwd`         — bare redirect with no command — opens for write, truncates, closes (zero-byte file).
- `: > /etc/passwd`       — null builtin then redirect (same effect, common idiom).
- `echo > /etc/passwd`    — any command with no output and a `>` redirect.
- `echo "x" > /etc/passwd` — replaces content with "x\n" (still data destruction).
- `cat /dev/null > /etc/passwd` — same.

These all bypass dcg today: there's no rm/find/truncate keyword, so the pack doesn't even run.

## Design

This is genuinely tricky because `>` and the target path may not be adjacent in the original command (whitespace varies; bash also accepts `1>file`, `2>file`, `&>file`, `>>file` (append, less destructive), `>|file` (force overwrite)).

Strategy: a NEW dedicated rule in `core.filesystem`:

- Keywords: `">"`, `">>"`, `">|"`, `"&>"`. (These need careful handling — `>` is in EVERY redirect, so the pack must re-run on lots of commands. Alternative: register `">/"` and `">$"` as more selective keywords.)

Better strategy: register the keyword as the path prefix `">/"` (literal `>/`) which appears whenever `>` redirects to a path starting with `/`. This catches `>/etc/passwd`, `> /etc/passwd` (after token normalization), etc. Caveat: also matches inside arguments like `"hello>/world"` — we'll lean on the destructive regex to disambiguate.

Destructive rule `redirect-truncate-root-home` (Critical):
- Regex: matches `(?:>|>\|)` (NOT `>>`) followed by optional whitespace, optional quote, then a sensitive path. Negative lookbehind to ensure NOT preceded by `>` (so `>>` is excluded).
- Pattern (rough): `(?<![<>])>\|?\s*['"]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|bin|sbin|root|boot|lib|lib64|var|home|sys|proc|dev|opt)/?\b|/(?:\s|$|['"])|~(?=\s|$|/)|\$\{?HOME\b)`

Destructive rule `redirect-truncate-general` (High):
- Same shape but matches any path (not just sensitive). Many commands legitimately redirect to non-temp files (e.g. `make > build.log`), so this rule needs careful safe-pattern coverage to avoid being too noisy.

Safe pattern `redirect-truncate-tmp` (whole-command anchored, optional):
- Allow `>` redirect into `/tmp/...`, `/var/tmp/...`, `$TMPDIR`, etc.
- Allow `>>` (append) — non-destructive, covered by negative lookbehind.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] redirect-truncate-root-home and -general rules ship.
- [ ] Negative lookbehind correctly excludes `>>` (append) — has unit test.
- [ ] `>|` (force-overwrite) is treated identically to `>`.
- [ ] `&>` (stdout+stderr to file) is treated identically.
- [ ] Numbered FDs (`1>`, `2>`, `1>|`) are treated identically.
- [ ] Append (`>>`) is NOT blocked (less destructive — only adds; explicit unit test).
- [ ] No regression on the rm-rf and find-delete suites.
- [ ] `scenario_redirect` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `> /etc/passwd`
- `: > /etc/passwd`
- `echo > /etc/shadow`
- `echo \"x\" > /etc/passwd`
- `cat /dev/null > /etc/passwd`
- `>| /etc/passwd`
- `2> /etc/passwd`
- `&> /etc/passwd`
- `1>| /etc/passwd`
- `(> /etc/passwd)`
- `echo done; > /etc/passwd`

Block (High):
- `> ./important.db`
- `make > build.log` ← THIS IS THE TENSION. We probably should NOT block this (very common workflow). Decide in design phase: maybe skip the -general tier entirely and only have root-home? Then everyone with a build.log is happy.

Allow (append, non-destructive):
- `echo line >> /etc/syslog`     ← append, NOT destructive
- `echo line >> ~/.bashrc`
- `make >> build.log`

Allow (temp):
- `> /tmp/scratch`
- `: > /tmp/cache`
- `echo data > $TMPDIR/file`

Allow (read redirects):
- `cat < /etc/passwd`           — read, not write
- `wc -l < /etc/hosts`

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 > /etc/passwd` MUST still block.

### Tension to resolve in design

The `-general` (High) tier has a real false-positive problem: `make > build.log`, `cargo test > test.log`, etc. are legitimate. Options:

(a) Skip the `-general` tier entirely. Only block `>` to sensitive system paths. This is the safest in terms of UX but misses `> ./database.db`.

(b) Add a default-allow safe pattern for `> <relative-path-with-extension-like-.log|.txt|.out>`. Risk: log file in CWD might be sensitive too.

(c) Block all `>` to non-temp paths but make it WARN-level (not deny-level) by default, so users see a warning on the first use and can allow-once. Severity = Medium.

Recommendation: (a) for v1 — only Critical-tier root-home matches. File a separate bead for the `-general` tier once we have telemetry on real false-positive rates.

### Out of scope

- `dd of=/etc/passwd` — covered by sibling bead.
- `tee /etc/passwd` — separate vector; covered by yet another sibling? File a separate bead if we want to cover it.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:03:16.030583799Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T22:11:53.156816847Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.4","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:03:16.030583799Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.5","title":"Block dd if=/dev/zero|urandom of=<sensitive-path> as truncate-equivalent","description":"## Description

`dd if=/dev/zero of=<file>` and `dd if=/dev/urandom of=<file>` write zeros or garbage into a file/device, destroying its contents. Today dcg has no `dd` coverage in the default packs.

The `system.disk` pack covers `dd` to /dev/* devices but is NOT default-enabled. This bead's scope is FILES (not devices) — file-level data destruction with sensitive targets like /etc/passwd. The system.disk-promotion question is tracked separately.

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"dd"` and `"/dd"` in `create_pack` and `PACK_ENTRIES`. Note: `dd` is two characters and very common as a substring (e.g. `address`, `delete`, `add`); rely on the regex's `\bdd\b` word boundary.
- PATH_NORMALIZER: add `dd` to capture group.
- Destructive `dd-overwrite-root-home` (Critical): regex `\bdd\b[^|;&]*\bof=['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|...)|/(?=\s|$|['"])|~|\$\{?HOME\b)`.
- Destructive `dd-overwrite-general` (High): `\bdd\b[^|;&]*\bof=<any-path>` not under temp.
- Safe `dd-tmp` (whole-command anchored): `^dd\s+[^|;&]*\bof=/tmp/...$` and the standard temp variants.

Note: `dd if=<file> of=/dev/null` (read-only operation, output discarded) should NOT be blocked. The destructive rule keys on `of=` to a real path, not `/dev/null`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 2 destructive + 4 safe patterns.
- [ ] Keyword in BOTH places.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated + unit-tested.
- [ ] Suggestion entries.
- [ ] `dd of=/dev/null` is NOT blocked (explicit unit test for the negative case).
- [ ] `dd if=...` (no of=) is NOT blocked.
- [ ] No regression: `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] `scenario_dd` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd`
- `dd if=/dev/urandom of=/etc/shadow`
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd bs=1M count=10`
- `dd of=/etc/passwd if=/dev/zero`     (operand order)
- `/usr/bin/dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd`
- `sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd`
- `echo done; dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd`

Block (High):
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=./important.db`

Allow:
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/scratch.bin bs=1M count=10`
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=$TMPDIR/cache.bin`
- `dd if=/etc/passwd of=/dev/null`            (read-only, output discarded)
- `dd if=/etc/passwd of=/tmp/passwd.bak`     (backup TO tmp from sensitive — NOT destruction; explicit unit test)
- `dd --help`
- `cat dd-script.sh`                          (substring trap)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 dd if=/dev/zero of=/etc/passwd` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness.)

### Out of scope

- `dd of=/dev/sda` and partition-table writes — system.disk pack territory; tracked by promote-system-disk bead.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:03:47.930487304Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T22:11:53.309953343Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.5","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:03:47.930487304Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.6","title":"Block tar --remove-files on sensitive sources as recursive-force-delete equivalent","description":"## Description

`tar --remove-files -cf <archive> <path>` first archives the listed paths into <archive>, then deletes the originals. With a sensitive source, this is a recursive-force-delete masquerading as an archive operation. Today dcg has no `tar` coverage.

Variants:
- `tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc`     — archive then delete /etc
- `tar --remove-files -czf out.tar.gz /etc` — same, gzipped
- `tar -cf out.tar --remove-files /etc`     — flag order varies
- `tar --remove-files -cf /dev/null /etc`   — delete-only (discard the archive)

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"tar"` and `"/tar"` in BOTH places. (Likely already partly handled by other tar-related packs but verify.)
- PATH_NORMALIZER: add `tar` to capture group.
- Destructive `tar-remove-files-root-home` (Critical): regex `\btar\b[^|;&]*\s--remove-files\b[^|;&]*` + sensitive path token.
- Destructive `tar-remove-files-general` (High): same but for any non-temp path.
- Safe `tar-remove-files-tmp`: `^tar\s+...--remove-files...\s+/tmp/...$`.

Note: the order of `--remove-files` and the source-path argument is variable, so the regex must be order-agnostic.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 2 destructive + 4 safe patterns.
- [ ] Keyword in BOTH `create_pack` and `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated + unit-tested.
- [ ] Suggestion entries.
- [ ] No regression: `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] Order-agnostic flag/path placement is unit-tested.
- [ ] `scenario_tar_remove_files` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc`
- `tar -cf out.tar --remove-files /etc`
- `tar --remove-files -czf out.tar.gz /home/user`
- `tar --remove-files -cf /dev/null /etc`        — delete-only (discarded archive)
- `/bin/tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc`
- `sudo tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc`

Block (High):
- `tar --remove-files -cf out.tar ./build`
- `tar --remove-files -cf out.tar important.db`

Allow:
- `tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /tmp/scratch`
- `tar -cf out.tar /etc`                         — NO --remove-files, archive only
- `tar -xf in.tar`                                — extract, not destruction
- `tar -tf in.tar`                                — list, not destruction
- `tar --help`
- `cat tar-readme.md`                             (substring trap)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness.)

### Out of scope

- `tar -xf evil.tar -C /` (path traversal in tarballs) — separate bead, not equivalence.
- `cpio --remove-files-after` if it exists — sibling bead if the syntax matters.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:04:14.492676801Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-29T02:08:52.045947469Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:04:14.492676801Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

- `tar --help`

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness.)

### Out of scope

- `cpio --remove-files-after` if it exists — sibling bead if the syntax matters.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

Order-agnostic flag/source placement, subshell terminator, sudo/env/path-prefix wrappers, and bypass-var guard all covered.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.6","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:04:14.492676801Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.7","title":"Block mv of sensitive sources (cross-segment recursive-force-delete bypass)","description":"## Description

Cross-segment bypass: `mv /etc /tmp/x && rm -rf /tmp/x`. Each segment is INDIVIDUALLY allowed by dcg today:
- `mv /etc /tmp/x` — mv to /tmp is fine (no rule blocks `mv` source-to-target).
- `rm -rf /tmp/x` — rm-rf in /tmp is allowed.

But the COMBINED effect is destruction of /etc. Same shape applies to `mv /etc /dev/null`, `mv /home/user /tmp/$$ && find /tmp/$$ -delete`, etc.

Today dcg has no cross-segment evaluation. This bead tracks the design and implementation of cross-segment destructive flow analysis.

## Design

This is a substantial new feature, not a regex update. Two viable approaches:

### Approach A: Block all `mv` of sensitive sources (regardless of destination)

- Add a `core.filesystem` destructive rule: `mv-sensitive-source-root-home`.
- Regex matches `\bmv\s+[^|;&]*['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|...)|...)` — any mv whose source is a sensitive path.
- Severity: Critical (the source is being moved away from its semantic location).
- Caveat: `mv /etc/hosts /etc/hosts.bak` (rename within /etc) would also match. Probably acceptable — renaming files in /etc is a legitimate operation but rare and reversible only if you remember to mv back. Block by default; allow-once for legit renames.

### Approach B: Cross-segment static analysis

- Build a small data-flow analyzer that tracks "tainted paths" across segments. After `mv X Y`, mark Y as "previously was X". If Y is then `rm -rf`'d, treat the rm as targeting X.
- Substantial new code. Likely worth a separate epic.

Recommendation: ship Approach A in the immediate next milestone (low complexity, high coverage). File Approach B as a follow-up epic for the more general cross-segment analysis problem (also covers `cp -al X Y && rm -rf X`, `ln -s X Y && rm -rf $(readlink Y)`, etc.).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `core.filesystem:mv-sensitive-source-root-home` (Critical) ships.
- [ ] Keyword `"mv"` and `"/mv"` in BOTH `create_pack` and `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated for `mv`.
- [ ] Suggestion entries explain the workaround (use `cp` for backup, then verify, then `rm`).
- [ ] Safe whitelist for in-place renames within /etc/usr/etc when destination is the same dir. (Optional — see decision below.)
- [ ] `scenario_mv_bypass` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.
- [ ] Approach B filed as a separate follow-up bead with notes on the data-flow analyzer scope.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `mv /etc /tmp/x && rm -rf /tmp/x`              (canonical bypass)
- `mv /etc /dev/null`                            (silent destruction)
- `mv /home/user /tmp/relocated`
- `mv $HOME /tmp/x`
- `sudo mv /etc /tmp/etc`
- `mv /etc/passwd /tmp/passwd-deleted`

Allow:
- `mv ./old.txt ./new.txt`                       (no sensitive source)
- `mv /tmp/foo /tmp/bar`                         (both temp)
- `mv build/output.bin dist/`                    (relative source)
- `mv --help`
- `cat mv-script.sh`                             (substring trap)

DECISION NEEDED:
- `mv /etc/hosts /etc/hosts.bak` — block or allow? Recommend BLOCK in v1 (rare operation; allow-once for legit cases). Document in suggestion.

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 mv /etc /tmp/x` MUST still block.

### Out of scope (file separately)

- `cp -al /etc /tmp/x && rm -rf /tmp/x` — same shape, but cp is benign. Approach B (data-flow) needed.
- `ln -s /etc /tmp/x && rm -rf /tmp/x/.` — symlink + delete-via-symlink.
- `chown -R nobody /etc` — different vector (permission destruction). Separate bead in `system.permissions`.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- No regression in existing test suite.

- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:04:53.549293360Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T22:11:53.620560461Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.7","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:04:53.549293360Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.8","title":"Promote system.disk to default-enabled (mkfs/dd-to-/dev/fdisk are critical-tier)","description":"## Description

The `system.disk` pack covers `mkfs`, `dd to /dev/*`, `fdisk`, `parted`, `mdadm`, `dmsetup`, `lvm` (`pvremove`/`vgremove`/`lvremove`/`lvreduce`/`pvmove`), `nbd-client`, and btrfs filesystem operations. None of these are recoverable; many are catastrophic.

Today `system.disk` is OPT-IN — users must explicitly enable it. For a tool whose sole purpose is preventing destructive commands, that is the wrong default. A first-time dcg user with default config has NO protection against `mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda` or `dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sdb`.

## Design

Promote `system.disk` to the default-enabled pack set:

1. In `src/config.rs`, add `system.disk` to the `Default::default()` value of `packs.enabled`.
2. Update `src/packs/mod.rs::PackEntry` for system.disk to mark `tier: 1` (was likely 5+) so it's evaluated alongside core.git/core.filesystem.
3. Update README.md to list system.disk as default-enabled (it currently lists only core.git, core.filesystem, database.postgresql, containers.docker — verify which are actually default).
4. Update CHANGELOG.md with a [BREAKING] note: users who previously had system.disk explicitly disabled in their config will keep that setting; users on default config get extra protection on next launch.
5. Verify all existing system.disk patterns still pass tests (no regression).
6. Add a default-config sanity test in `src/config.rs::tests` that asserts `system.disk` is in the default enabled set.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `system.disk` is in `Config::default().packs.enabled`.
- [ ] `dcg packs` (no config) shows `system.disk` with a checkmark.
- [ ] All existing `system.disk` unit tests pass.
- [ ] Default config still allows safe operations (smoke test: simple `ls`, `git status`, `cargo build` etc. produce empty stdout).
- [ ] README.md and CHANGELOG.md updated.
- [ ] `scenario_system_disk_default` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh` verifies that `mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1` is BLOCKED on a default-config invocation.
- [ ] No double-blocking: ensure no command that's already blocked by core.* is also blocked twice with confusing messaging.

## Notes

### Test scenarios (must all be in scenario_system_disk_default)

Block (default config, no env vars, no custom config):
- `mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1`
- `mkswap /dev/sdb`
- `fdisk /dev/sda`
- `parted /dev/sda mklabel gpt`
- `dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sda bs=1M`
- `mdadm --zero-superblock /dev/sda`
- `lvremove vg0/lv0`
- `pvremove /dev/sda`
- `wipefs -a /dev/sda`

Still allow (default config):
- `lsblk`
- `df -h`
- `mount | grep ext4`
- `parted -l`                                     (list, not modify)
- `cat /proc/partitions`
- `lvs`, `vgs`, `pvs`                             (list, not modify)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1` MUST still block (these are CRITICAL — bypass var must not relax).

### Migration impact

- Existing users on default config will see new blocks on their NEXT dcg invocation after upgrade. This is intentional. Mention in CHANGELOG with a clear "if this disrupts your workflow, add `disabled = [\"system.disk\"]` to ~/.config/dcg/config.toml".
- Users who explicitly disabled system.disk in their config keep it disabled (no override).","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:05:30.235850858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T22:11:53.772320559Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.8","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:05:30.235850858Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id": "git_safety_guard-nuyf", "title": "[Epic 5] Context-Aware Allowlisting (Path-Specific Rules)", "description": "## EPIC 5: Context-Aware Allowlisting (Path-Specific Rules)

### Background & Context
Current allowlists are binary: a command is either allowed everywhere or blocked everywhere. This doesn't match reality:
- 'rm -rf node_modules' is fine in /home/user/projects/myapp
- 'rm -rf node_modules' is NOT fine in / or /etc
- 'DROP DATABASE test_db' is fine in dev environment paths

Users must choose between blocking (friction) or allowing everywhere (risk).

### Goals & Objectives
1. Extend allowlist config to support path conditions
2. Match paths using glob patterns (for flexibility)
3. Maintain backward compatibility (simple allowlists still work)
4. Clear, intuitive config syntax

### Reasoning & Justification
This is ranked #5 because it solves the PRECISION problem:
- Enables least-privilege allowlisting
- Reduces over-permissive configs
- Matches real-world usage patterns
- Foundation for other features (suggestions, temporary allowlists)

### Config Schema Design

CURRENT (keep for backward compat):
allowlist = ["npm run build", "cargo test"]

NEW (extended format):
[[allowlist_rules]]
pattern = "rm -rf node_modules"
paths = ["/home/*/projects/*", "/workspace/*"]
comment = "Allow node_modules cleanup in project directories"

### Implementation Considerations
- Config parsing: src/config.rs needs new AllowlistRule struct
- AllowlistRule: { pattern: String, paths: Vec<String>, comment: Option<String> }
- Path matching: Use glob crate
- Evaluation: In allowlist check, also check if CWD matches any path pattern
- Backward compat: Parse old format into AllowlistRule with paths = ["*"]

### Path Matching Semantics
- '*' matches any single path component
- '**' matches zero or more path components
- Paths are matched against CWD at evaluation time
- If no paths specified, defaults to ["*"]

### Success Criteria
- Simple allowlists continue to work unchanged
- Path-specific rules correctly match CWD
- Glob patterns work as expected
- Config validation catches invalid patterns

### Related Codebase Files
- src/config.rs (AllowlistRule struct, parsing)
- src/evaluator.rs (allowlist evaluation with path check)

### Dependencies
- None - this is foundational for allowlist improvements

### Project Goal Alignment
This serves the 'robust, reliable' goals by enabling precise security policies. Principle of least privilege applied to command allowlisting.", "status": "closed", "priority": 1, "issue_type": "epic", "owner": "jeff141421@gmail.com", "created_at": "2026-01-18T18:09:33.817771817Z", "created_by": "Dicklesworthstone", "updated_at": "2026-01-19T08:34:52.927906572Z", "closed_at": "2026-01-19T08:34:52.927805281Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "labels": ["core"]}

@@ -15,6 +15,12 @@ Post-v0.4.3 work on `main` that has not yet been tagged.
 
 ### Security Hardening
 
+- **Recursive-force-delete bypass family** (`core.filesystem`): closed five sibling-bypass families an agent could use after `rm -rf` is blocked.
+  - `find ... -delete` on sensitive paths (Critical/High) — closes the `find -delete` path-bypass plus compound, subshell, and path-prefix variants.
+  - `unlink <sensitive>` (Critical/High) — POSIX unlink(2) primitive.
+  - `truncate -s 0|--size=0|-s -N` on sensitive paths (Critical/High) — in-place zero/shrink.
+  - `shred [-u|--remove|-fzu] <sensitive>` (Critical/High) — DoD-style overwrite + optional unlink.
+  - `tar --remove-files` on sensitive sources (Critical/High) — archive-then-delete masquerading as an archive operation; order-agnostic flag/source placement; `tar --remove-files -cf /dev/null /etc` (delete-only) blocked.
 - **Strict git pack**: expanded dangerous-command detection for additional destructive git patterns ([6d950f3](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/commit/6d950f3), [031e84a](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/commit/031e84a))
 - Removed safe patterns in strict git pack that created a compound-command bypass ([d6ce202](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/commit/d6ce202))
 - Podman `rm`/`rmi` combined-flag bypass (e.g. `podman rm -af`) ([d9d23b5](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/commit/d9d23b5))

@@ -508,10 +508,81 @@ scenario_shred_bypass_var() {
     assert_allowed_under_truthy_bypass 'shred -u /etc/passwd'
 }
 
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# tar --remove-files (git_safety_guard-nqhi.6)
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+scenario_tar_remove_files_root_home() {
+    # Flag-then-source.
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'              'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -czf out.tar.gz /home/user'    'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /usr/local'        'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Source-then-flag (order-agnostic).
+    assert_blocked 'tar -cf out.tar --remove-files /etc'              'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'tar -cf out.tar /etc --remove-files'              'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Delete-only (archive discarded to /dev/null).
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf /dev/null /etc'            'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Quoted sensitive paths.
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar "/etc"'            'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked "tar --remove-files -cf out.tar '/etc'"            'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Home variants.
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar ~/.ssh'            'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar $HOME/.aws'        'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar ${HOME}/.gnupg'    'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Compound forms (\btar\b matches at any boundary).
+    assert_blocked 'echo done; tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'   'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'true && tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'      'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked '(tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc)'            'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Wrappers (sudo/env stripped).
+    assert_blocked 'sudo tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'         'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'env FOO=bar tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'  'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Path-prefixed (PATH_NORMALIZER).
+    assert_blocked '/usr/bin/tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'     'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked '/bin/tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'         'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Mixed-source: a /tmp source does NOT rescue when an /etc co-source
+    # is also present — root-home must still fire.
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /tmp/foo /etc/bar' 'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home' 'critical'
+}
+
+scenario_tar_remove_files_general() {
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar ./build'           'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-general' 'high'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar important.db'      'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-general' 'high'
+    assert_blocked 'tar -cf out.tar --remove-files data.json'         'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-general' 'high'
+    assert_blocked 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /data/important'   'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-general' 'high'
+}
+
+scenario_tar_remove_files_temp_safe() {
+    assert_allowed 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /tmp/scratch'
+    assert_allowed 'tar -cf out.tar --remove-files /tmp/foo'
+    assert_allowed 'tar --remove-files -czf out.tar.gz /var/tmp/cache'
+    assert_allowed 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar $TMPDIR/scratch'
+    assert_allowed 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar ${TMPDIR}/scratch'
+}
+
+scenario_tar_no_false_positive() {
+    # No --remove-files means no destruction trigger.
+    assert_allowed 'tar -cf out.tar /etc'
+    assert_allowed 'tar -czf out.tar.gz /home/user'
+    assert_allowed 'tar -xf in.tar'
+    assert_allowed 'tar -xzf in.tar.gz -C /tmp'
+    assert_allowed 'tar -tf in.tar'
+    assert_allowed 'tar --help'
+    assert_allowed 'tar --version'
+    # Substring traps.
+    assert_allowed 'cat tar-readme.md'
+    assert_allowed 'ls /etc/tar-config'
+    # `--remove-files` mentioned but not as a tar flag (no `tar` invocation).
+    assert_allowed 'echo --remove-files'
+}
+
+scenario_tar_remove_files_bypass_var() {
+    assert_blocked_under_falsy_bypass  'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'      'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home'
+    assert_blocked_under_falsy_bypass  'tar --remove-files -cf /dev/null /etc'    'core.filesystem:tar-remove-files-root-home'
+    assert_allowed_under_truthy_bypass 'tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc'
+}
+
 # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
 # (placeholders — implementers replace with concrete assertions)
 # scenario_dd_*() { :; }
-# scenario_tar_remove_files_*() { :; }
 # scenario_redirect_*() { :; }
 # scenario_mv_sensitive_*() { :; }
 # scenario_system_disk_default() { :; }

@@ -1285,9 +1285,17 @@ fn create_destructive_patterns() -> Vec<DestructivePattern> {
         // this rule because the regex doesn't position-parse `-cf`'s
         // argument. Accepted: writing tar archives to /etc is itself
         // suspicious and `dcg allow-once` covers the rare legitimate case.
+        // Path-tail terminator set includes `)` (in addition to the
+        // standard `\s|$|['"]`) so a subshell form like
+        // `(tar --remove-files -cf out.tar /etc)` — where /etc is the
+        // last token before the closing paren — still classifies as
+        // Critical (root-home) rather than falling through to the
+        // High-tier general rule. The other sibling rules (rm-rf,
+        // find-delete, unlink, truncate-zero, shred) have the same
+        // latent gap; closing it pack-wide is tracked separately.
         destructive_pattern!(
             "tar-remove-files-root-home",
-            r#"\btar\b[^|;&]*?\s--remove-files\b[^|;&]*?(?:\s|=)['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|bin|sbin|root|boot|lib|lib64|var|home|sys|proc|dev|opt)(?:/|(?=\s|$|['"]))|/(?=\s|$|['"])|~(?=\s|$|/)|\$\{?HOME\b)|\btar\b[^|;&]*?(?:\s|=)['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|bin|sbin|root|boot|lib|lib64|var|home|sys|proc|dev|opt)(?:/|(?=\s|$|['"]))|/(?=\s|$|['"])|~(?=\s|$|/)|\$\{?HOME\b)[^|;&]*?\s--remove-files\b"#,
+            r#"\btar\b[^|;&]*?\s--remove-files\b[^|;&]*?(?:\s|=)['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|bin|sbin|root|boot|lib|lib64|var|home|sys|proc|dev|opt)(?:/|(?=[\s\)'"]|$))|/(?=[\s\)'"]|$)|~(?=\s|$|/|\))|\$\{?HOME\b)|\btar\b[^|;&]*?(?:\s|=)['"\\]?(?:/(?:etc|usr|bin|sbin|root|boot|lib|lib64|var|home|sys|proc|dev|opt)(?:/|(?=[\s\)'"]|$))|/(?=[\s\)'"]|$)|~(?=\s|$|/|\))|\$\{?HOME\b)[^|;&]*?\s--remove-files\b"#,
             "tar --remove-files on a sensitive system or home path is recursive deletion masquerading as an archive operation. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS.",
             Critical,
             "`tar --remove-files -cf <archive> <source>` first archives the source paths \
